content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
Phone: (916) 322-5660 • Fax: (916) 322-0886 
Dianne Jacob 
Chairwoman, Second District 
San Diego Board of Supervisors 
1600 Pacific Highway, Room 335 
San Diego, CA 92101 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-17-261 
Dear Ms. Jacob: 
November 9,2017 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of 
the Political Refonn Act ('•Act").1 
Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the 
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions, such as common law conflict of 
interest or Section 1090. Also, note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re 
Oglesby ( 1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71 ); any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and 
accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should 
contact us for additional advice. 
QUESTION 
Do you have a disqualifying conflict of interest in governmental decisions by the Board of 
Supervisors regarding the construction of an additional turning lane of an intersection located 
several miles away from your properties? 
CONCLUSION 
No. The construction of the additional lane will not have a reasonably foreseeable material 
effect on your properties. 
1 The Political Refonn Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


FACTS 
File No. A-17-261 
Page No. 2 
You are the elected County Supervisor for San Diego Supervisory District No. 2. District 2 
is a largely rural area spanning greater than 2,000 square miles of the south-eastern portion of San 
Diego County. 
You own your residence located in Jamul - an unincorporated area of San Diego County. 
Your residence is approximately 2.3 miles south east of the project. You also own rural property 
consisting of four contiguous parcels {"Ranch"), which are also located in Jamul. Combined, the 
Ranch parcels total approximately 90 acres. The Ranch is located approximately 14 miles south east 
of the project. 
The matter to be considered by the Board of Supervisors involves whether to establish 
appropriations and approve the advertisement and subsequent award of a construction contract for 
the project. The project solely involves an improvement to an existing intersection within an 
existing public right of way. Specifically, the project entails the construction of a second 
northbound left-tum lane on Steele Canyon Road, turning westbound onto Willow Glen Drive. The 
intersection is within the boundaries of the census-designated place of Rancho San Diego, adjacent 
to Jamul. The project will also include new curb ramps, pedestrian push buttons, and yellow school 
zone crosswalks. 
The project is intended to benefit both the Valle de Oro and Jamul communities by 
improving traffic flow and reducing congestion. There are approximately 37,000 average daily trips 
through the intersection that will be affected by the project. Generally, the intersection provides a 
connection to the Valle de Oro community for Jamul residents. State Route 94, which connects to 
Steele Canyon Road, is the main route to the Jamul community. This is also an intersection that you 
travel through when driving to work and on occasional personal trips. 
ANALYSIS 
Under Section 87100, a conflict of interest exists whenever a public official makes, 
participates in making, or uses his or her position to influence a governmental decision that has a 
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on one or more of the official's interests.· 
Section 87103{b) indicates that an official has an interest in "[ a ]ny real property in which 
the public official has a direct or indirect interest of at least $2,000." Your real property interests in 
your residence and the Ranch are the only interests implicated by the decisions. Accordingly, we 
must determine if the decisions will have a foreseeable and material effect. 
Foreseeability: 
The standard for foreseeability differs depending on whether an interest is explicitly 
involved in the decisions. (Regulation 18701.) An interest is explicitly involved in a decision if the 
interest is a named party in, or the subject of, the governmental decision. {Regulation 18701 (a).) 
Under the facts you have provided, neither your residence nor the Ranch are explicitly involved in 
the decisions. Thus, Regulation 18701 (h) provides: "[i]n general, if the financial effect can be 


File No. A-17-261 
Page No. 3 
recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical, it is reasonably 
foreseeable. "2 
Materiality: 
The financial effects of project-related decisions on your real property interests are material 
if the decisions: 
• Would cause a reasonably prudent person, using due care and consideration under the 
circumstances, to believe that the governmental decision was of such a nature that its 
reasonably foreseeable financial effect would influence the market value of the official's 
property (Regulation 18702.2(a)(l2.) 
The project entails adding a second left-tum lane at the intersection of Steele Canyon Road 
and Willow Glen Drive. Your residence and Ranch are located in the adjacent community of Jamul, 
approximately 2.3 and 14 miles away, respectfully. Irrespective of the fact that your work and 
personal commutes may benefit from the additional lane, it is not foreseeable that the market value 
of your properties will be materially affected. As such, you are not disqualified from project-related 
decisions. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
RPOC:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Brian G. Lau 
Assistant General Counsel 
 lo:U-C-
Ryan P. O'Connor 
By: 
Counsel, Legal Division 
2 Regulation 18701 (b) also provides a non-exhaustive list of six factors that may be considered in the 
"reasonably foreseeable" determination.
